visits.text: At dawn in Białowieża Forest, silence carries for miles. This forest, formed 10,000 years ago during the last glaciation, has never been industrially exploited—it's been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1979 and recognized as a biosphere reserve since 1976. It stretches across 141,885 hectares straddling the Polish-Belarusian border. The strictly protected zone can only be explored with a licensed guide; advance booking is essential in season.

The Białowieża National Park Museum presents the forest's natural history, the reintroduction of bison starting in 1952—the last wild specimens had been killed in 1919—and the geography of the massif. The adjacent bison reserve allows you to observe them in a semi-open space, along with tarpan horses, lynx and wolves.

activities.text: Guided excursion into the primeval forest: the Polish section of the park harbors around 800 European bison, the largest terrestrial mammal on the continent (an adult male weighs up to 840 kg). Licensed guides know their habits; the best observation times are early morning or late afternoon in the clearings. Bike excursions in the landscape protection zone are possible without a guide.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Marvel at Branicki Palace, often called the 'Versailles of the North,' an impressive baroque residence surrounded by meticulously maintained French-style gardens. This 18th-century aristocratic palace features ornate interiors, grand staircases, and beautiful grounds complete with fountains and sculpted hedges. The palace now serves as a medical university while remaining open for guided tours.

Visit the Orthodox Church of Saints Peter and Paul, a stunning example of Byzantine-influenced religious architecture reflecting the region's multicultural heritage. The church's distinctive domes and richly decorated interior showcase the Orthodox traditions that have flourished in northeastern Poland for centuries. This spiritual landmark provides insight into the diverse religious tapestry of the borderland region.

Białystok's Branicki Palace was transformed into a baroque residence between 1728 and 1752 by Jan Klemens Branicki, Crown Grand Hetman, who harbored ambitions of becoming King of Poland. The complex—palace, formal French gardens, orangery, arsenal—earned him the sobriquet 'Versailles of Podlachia.' Seventy percent damaged during World War II, it was reconstructed to its original design between 1946 and 1962 and now houses the Medical University. The gardens are freely accessible; the Great Hall, renowned for its acoustics, hosts international sacred music festivals.

A few minutes' walk away, the 17th-century Orthodox Church of Saint Nicholas stands as a reminder that Białystok is the capital of a region where Catholics, Orthodox and Protestants long coexisted. Its austere façade contrasts sharply with the baroque exuberance of the neighboring palace.

activities.text: Relax in Białystok City Park, one of the oldest and most beautiful urban parks in Poland, perfect for a peaceful afternoon stroll or picnic. The park's tree-lined avenues, ornamental ponds, and historic monuments create a tranquil green oasis in the heart of the city. This beloved local gathering place offers a glimpse into everyday Polish life away from typical tourist routes.

Journey into the primeval Białowieża Forest, one of the last remaining ancient woodlands in Europe and a UNESCO World Heritage Site straddling the Polish-Belarusian border. This remarkable forest shelters rare European bison, lynx, elk, and countless other species in an ecosystem virtually unchanged for millennia. Wildlife sanctuaries, educational centers, and forest trails allow visitors to experience this unique natural treasure.

Walking through the historic center built under Branicki: town hall, archives, old school—Jan Klemens Branicki essentially designed the entire town in the 18th century. The market square and its cobbled streets preserve that urban coherence.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Discover Bydgoszcz (formerly Bromberg), a charming city where waterways weave through the historic center creating a unique urban landscape often called Little Berlin. The picturesque Mill Island (Wyspa Młyńska) features restored granaries, elegant bridges and peaceful canals that invite leisurely exploration. This former Prussian city blends German and Polish architectural influences in its well-preserved historic quarter.

Stroll along the Brda River waterfront, where historic red-brick granaries reflect in the calm waters and colorful facades line the embankments. The city's musical heritage is celebrated through numerous monuments and the renowned Opera Nova concert hall. Bydgoszcz offers a quieter, authentic Polish city experience away from the crowds of more famous destinations.

activities.text: Take a boat trip through the city's canals to appreciate its unique waterway network and historic warehouse district

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Wander through the Hortulus themed garden complex in Dobrzyca, a place bursting with colors, fragrances and horticultural wonders. Children can navigate the challenging hedge maze while parents admire the beautifully designed Japanese gardens, tranquil water features and artistic rock gardens. This peaceful oasis develops appreciation for nature's beauty and offers a perfect family retreat.

Test your navigation skills in the seasonal Corn Maze, a huge labyrinth created each summer in the fields of Dobrzyca. Finding the way out of this natural puzzle provides exciting fun and a genuine challenge for visitors of all ages. The maze design changes every year, often incorporating additional surprises and attractions hidden within its winding paths.

activities.text: Relax in the garden cafes and enjoy playgrounds designed for family recreation amid beautiful landscapes

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Relax on the beautiful wide beach of Dziwnów, a charming seaside town perfect for family holidays by the Baltic Sea. The golden sands invite children to build sandcastles, splash in the waves and enjoy supervised beach activities during the summer season. Organized animations, competitions and events keep young visitors entertained throughout the day.

Explore Dziwnów's picturesque fishing port, where traditional cutters and cruise ships create an authentic maritime atmosphere. Watching the fishermen bring in their daily catch offers a glimpse into the time-honored traditions of Baltic fishing communities. The harbor promenade provides lovely walks with views of the boats bobbing gently in the harbor.

activities.text: Enjoy sea bathing and beach activities in this family-friendly resort with excellent facilities for children

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Built by the Teutonic Knights starting in 1274, Malbork Castle sprawls across 21 hectares—more than 4.5 million red bricks arranged in three nested defensive rings. In 1309, the Grand Master relocated his seat here from Venice, making the castle the capital of a monastic state that stretched along the entire Baltic coast. It's the largest brick castle in the world, a UNESCO site since 1997. Allow at least 2.5 hours with the GPS audio guide (available in English).

St. Mary's Basilica in Gdańsk ranks among the largest brick churches in the world: 105 meters long with a capacity of 25,000 people. The 15th-century astronomical clock, restored after WWII, tracks the liturgical calendar and celestial movements. The tower offers sweeping views over the old town and harbor.

The Great Crane (Żuraw), built in the 15th century on the Motława quay, is the largest surviving medieval crane in Europe. Two treadwheels, each 6 meters in diameter, powered the hoisting cables—the mechanism could lift loads up to 4 tons to equip ships with masts. Today it houses the Maritime Museum.

Mariacka Street connects St. Mary's Basilica to the waterfront. Its 17th- and 18th-century townhouses with sculpted stone stoops shelter goldsmiths' workshops specializing in amber—the fossilized resin extracted from the Baltic seabed, worked in the region since antiquity. The Golden Gate (17th century) marks the eastern entrance to the city.

On September 1, 1939, at 4:45 AM, the German battleship Schleswig-Holstein opened fire on the Polish garrison at Westerplatte. This was the first battle of World War II. 182 Polish soldiers held the peninsula for 7 days against 3,500 Germans backed by aviation and naval artillery, surrendering on September 7 only when their ammunition ran out. The site preserves the ruins of the barracks, a 1966 monument, and a museum. Accessible by car or boat from the Motława quay (1 hr round trip, recommended option).

The Museum of the Second World War (Muzeum II Wojny Światowej) opened in 2017 in a striking contemporary building. Across 2,300 m² of permanent exhibition, it recounts the conflict on a global scale from the Polish perspective—testimonies, original artifacts, archives, and reconstructions. Allow at least 2.5 hours.

The European Solidarity Centre (Europejskie Centrum Solidarności, opened 2014) occupies the site of the former Lenin Shipyards, where Lech Wałęsa and workers founded Solidarność in August 1980. The permanent exhibition traces the struggle against the communist regime and the fall of the Wall. The Gdańsk Agreements, signed August 31, 1980, are inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World register.

Oliwa Cathedral, north of Gdańsk, is the longest Cistercian church in the world: 107 meters long by 19 meters wide. Its Baroque organ was built between 1763 and 1788 by Cistercian monk Johann Wilhelm Wulff; it contains 7,876 pipes, 5 keyboards, and a rococo case in the shape of a horseshoe adorned with small mechanical angels that animate during play. Twenty-minute concerts are held hourly in summer. Oliwa Park, behind the cathedral, is one of the finest gardens in Pomerania.

Stroll along Gdańsk's magnificent Royal Way, the ceremonial route where Polish kings once paraded past the city's grandest patrician mansions and civic monuments. The Main Town's colorful facades, ornate portals, and elegant gables create one of the most photogenic urban landscapes in all of Europe. Despite heavy wartime damage, meticulous reconstruction has restored this Hanseatic masterpiece to its former glory.

Explore Gdańsk's historic harbor, where the Motława River is lined with medieval brick warehouses and the iconic wooden crane that once loaded merchant ships. This atmospheric waterfront district captures the essence of the city's prosperous maritime past as one of the Baltic's most important trading centers. Boat cruises depart regularly, offering unique perspectives on the historic architecture from the water.

Visit Malbork Castle, the largest brick castle in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage site, easily reached on a day trip from Gdańsk. This massive 13th-century fortress, built by the Teutonic Knights, comprises three interconnected castle complexes surrounded by multiple defensive walls. The extensively restored interiors house fascinating collections of medieval armor, amber, and religious art.

Discover the seaside charm of Sopot, a fashionable resort town that boasts Europe's longest wooden pier extending over 500 meters into the Baltic Sea. This elegant spa town has attracted visitors since the 19th century with its beautiful beaches, tree-lined promenades, and distinctive architecture including the famous Crooked House. The lively atmosphere and seaside setting make Sopot a delightful escape from urban sightseeing.

Explore Gdynia, Poland's largest seaport, which grew from a small fishing village into a modern maritime hub during the interwar period. The Polish Naval Museum and preserved warships moored in the harbor tell the story of Poland's naval heritage. The city's modernist architecture and dynamic harbor promenade reflect its unique origins as a 20th-century planned city.

activities.text: Arrive in Gdańsk late afternoon from Malbork (55 km, ~45 min). First stroll along Długi Targ (the Long Market), the central axis of the old Hanseatic town. Neptune's Fountain (1633, attributed to Abraham van den Blocke) marks the heart of the square. Dinner in one of the historic cellars around Piwna Street.

Walk along the Motława, the canal that borders the old town. The Hanseatic merchants' mansions and granaries of Spichlerze Island reflect in the water. A ferry connects the quay to Ołowianka Island (a few minutes, frequent departures) where the Philharmonic stands.

Experience the Tri-City by combining visits to Gdańsk, Sopot, and Gdynia—three distinct yet interconnected urban centers along the Baltic coast. Efficient rail connections link these neighboring cities, each offering its own character: medieval Gdańsk, elegant Sopot, and maritime Gdynia. This urban trio provides a complete introduction to Poland's Baltic heritage and contemporary coastal life.

Gdynia, the third city of the Tri-City (12 km north of Sopot), was built from scratch between the two world wars to provide Poland with a seaport. Its 1920s–1930s architecture—Modernism and Art Deco—contrasts with Gdańsk's Gothic brick. The Emigration Museum, housed in the former maritime terminal of 1933, traces the waves of departures to the Americas. The destroyer ORP Błyskawica, a museum ship moored at the quay, is the oldest combat destroyer in the world.

Last shopping on Mariacka Street—amber workshops, Baltic spices, craft vodkas. Farewell dinner in a historic cellar in the old town: pickled herring (śledź), smoked Baltic fish, or cod in hazelnut butter.

Free morning in Gdańsk. Market at Hala Targowa on the right bank of the Motława (fresh produce, smoked fish, raw amber). Final stroll along the Motława quay and ferry to Ołowianka Island. Transfer to Gdańsk-Rębiechowo Airport (GDN), 15 km from the center.

Polish Baltic Coast: Gdańsk, Sopot & the Teutonic Fortress
Polish Baltic Coast: Gdańsk, Sopot & the Teutonic Fortress

visits.text: The Hel Peninsula is a 35-km-long sandbar, 200 meters to 3 km wide, separating Puck Bay from the open sea. The road runs sometimes along the beach, sometimes through pine forests. The villages of Chałupy, Kuźnica, and Jastarnia have preserved their fishermen's architecture. In summer, a transport option from Gdańsk is the Żegluga Gdańska catamaran (~1.5 hr crossing).

The Hel Seal Sanctuary (Fokarium) is a research and rehabilitation station for Baltic grey seals, run by the University of Gdańsk's Institute of Oceanography since 1999. The seals—an endangered Baltic species—are treated here before being released back to the sea. Feeding sessions take place two to three times daily and allow close-up observation of the animals.

activities.text: The kitesurfing and windsurfing spots at Chałupy and Kuźnica rank among the most renowned in Northern Europe, thanks to the prevailing westerly wind sweeping across the shallow bay. On the bay side, calm water suits beginners and families. On the open sea side, the beach is wilder and windier. The Hel lighthouse (41.5 m, 19th century) dominates the tip. WWII bunkers survive in the pine forest.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Polish Baltic Coast: Gdańsk, Sopot & the Teutonic Fortress

visits.text: Kazimierz Dolny has 3,600 inhabitants. Its early 17th-century Renaissance Rynek has been preserved almost intact for 400 years. The Przybyłów brothers' houses, commissioned in 1615 by two grain merchants, feature sculpted facades characteristic of the so-called "Lublin" Renaissance - a local style with Mannerist ornamentation. The town owes its medieval and Renaissance prosperity to the grain trade between Kraków and Gdańsk via the Vistula. Decline began when the river changed course in the 18th century.

The ruins of the Gothic castle, built in the 14th century under Casimir the Great, rise on the hill overlooking the town. The view from the battlements extends over the Rynek, the 16th-century granaries by the river and the Vistula meander. A cylindrical watchtower, older than the castle, completes the defensive ensemble.

activities.text: The Hill of Three Crosses (Góra Trzech Krzyży) offers the best panorama of the town and the Vistula - the crosses were erected in 1708 during a cholera epidemic. The local bakery specializing in koguty - rooster-shaped breads, a town specialty since the 19th century - is located on the Rynek or in adjacent streets. Kazimierz's art gallery brings together a hundred studios and exhibition spaces: painters and sculptors have been settling there since the 1920s.

days_plan.itin_title: Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula

visits.text: Discover the Museum of Sea Fishing in Niechorze, which presents the rich history of fishing along the Polish Baltic coast. Visitors can examine traditional fishing tools, intricate ship models and fascinating exhibits that document the challenging lives of generations of fishermen. Interactive displays and multimedia presentations bring maritime heritage to life in an engaging, accessible way.

Climb the nearby Niechorze Lighthouse for spectacular views across the coastline and surrounding landscape. This distinctive red-brick tower has guided ships along these waters for over a century and now welcomes visitors to appreciate its panoramic vistas. The lighthouse complex tells the story of maritime navigation along this historically important shipping route.

Dive into the fascinating underwater world at Kołobrzeg Oceanarium, where numerous aquariums showcase exotic fish, sharks, vibrant corals and mysterious sea creatures from around the globe. Interactive exhibitions and multimedia presentations reveal the secrets of ocean ecosystems, environmental threats and the importance of marine conservation. This educational adventure captivates visitors of all ages with the wonders hidden beneath the waves.

Explore the Polish Arms Museum, home to an impressive collection of military history spanning centuries of Polish armed forces. Children can marvel at various weapons, uniforms, military vehicles, aircraft and tanks displayed both indoors and in outdoor exhibition areas. Interactive exhibits and multimedia presentations make history accessible and exciting for young visitors.

Walk along the iconic Kołobrzeg Pier, stretching far out into the Baltic Sea and offering refreshing sea breezes and panoramic coastal views. The lighthouse nearby provides another vantage point from which to appreciate the beauty of this historic spa town. The seafront promenade buzzes with activity and creates perfect opportunities for a memorable seaside stroll.

activities.text: Walk along the coastal path connecting the lighthouse to the beach for scenic Baltic views
Watch reconstruction shows and participate in educational workshops at the Arms Museum

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Experience the Wild West at Western Park in Ustronie Morskie, a themed attraction that transports visitors to the American frontier. Children can meet cowboys and Native Americans, watch thrilling stunt shows and learn to shoot a bow and arrow. The park features playgrounds, a mini zoo and handicraft workshops that create lasting memories of adventure.

Cool off at Koszalin Aquapark, the perfect destination for water fun regardless of weather conditions. The modern facility features numerous slides, wave pools, lazy rivers and dedicated zones for the youngest swimmers. Adults can unwind in saunas, jacuzzis and relaxation areas while children splash and play in complete safety under qualified lifeguard supervision.

activities.text: Enjoy a full day of water attractions including slides, wave pools and family swimming areas

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

places.name: Krakow

visits.text: Perched atop Wawel Hill, the Royal Castle served as the residence of Polish monarchs from the 11th to the 17th century. The arcaded inner courtyard was redesigned in Renaissance style during the 16th century under Sigismund I, whose architect brought master craftsmen from Italy to Kraków. You'll tour the royal apartments, state rooms adorned with Flemish tapestries, and the royal treasury. Allow at least two hours for the entire complex.

Wawel Cathedral witnessed royal coronations from 1320 to 1734 and served as the final resting place for Polish sovereigns. Its crypts house the tombs of kings, heads of state, and national writers. The Sigismund Chapel, crowned by a golden dome, is considered the finest example of Renaissance funerary architecture in Central Europe. The great Sigismund Bell, cast in 1520, weighs approximately 13 tonnes.

The Rynek Główny, laid out in 1257, measures 200 metres per side - making it Europe's largest medieval market square. St. Mary's Basilica on the north side houses Veit Stoss's altarpiece, carved between 1477 and 1489: 13 metres tall with 200 figures carved from linden wood. The altarpiece wings open daily at 11:50am. The Cloth Hall (Sukiennice) at the centre of the square dates from the Renaissance period.

Kazimierz was founded in 1335 as an independent town by King Casimir the Great, before being incorporated into Kraków in 1791. From the 15th century onwards, the Jewish population made it one of the most important cultural and religious centres in Central Europe. Seven synagogues remain standing in the quarter. The Remuh Synagogue (1553), the smallest in the district, is one of only two still open for worship. Its adjoining cemetery, in use from 1552 to 1799, preserves Renaissance and Baroque tombstones that are among the best-preserved in Europe.

Oskar Schindler's Enamel Factory (Fabryka Emalia Oskara Schindlera) on Lipowa Street has housed the museum on Nazi occupation of Kraków since 2010. Schindler employed Jewish workers here during the war, thereby protecting over 1,200 people from deportation. The permanent exhibition covers life in the occupied city between 1939 and 1945: photographs, period objects, testimonies and multimedia installations. Not to be confused with Auschwitz-Birkenau - this museum specifically addresses Kraków itself.

The Wieliczka Salt Mine has produced salt since the 13th century. It appears on UNESCO's very first World Heritage List, established in 1978. The standard tourist route descends to 135m depth through 3km of galleries: underground lakes, sculptures carved from salt and, on level 3, St. Kinga's Chapel. Carved from rock salt between 1896 and 1935, this chapel measures 54m long, 18m wide and 12m high - its chandeliers, altars and bas-reliefs depicting the Last Supper are entirely made of rock salt. Online booking advised during high season.

Lose yourself in Kraków's magnificent Rynek Główny, Europe's largest medieval market square and the beating heart of this beloved former royal capital. This vast paved expanse, ringed by elegant burgher houses, Gothic churches and the Renaissance Cloth Hall, pulses with life from dawn until well past midnight. Horse-drawn carriages, street performers and café terraces create an atmosphere of timeless European elegance.

Climb to Wawel Castle, the former seat of Polish kings perched dramatically on a limestone outcrop above the Vistula. This magnificent complex of Gothic and Renaissance buildings houses state rooms, the royal private apartments and an impressive collection of Flemish tapestries and armour. The adjoining Wawel Cathedral, coronation site of Polish monarchs, contains the royal tombs and the silver shrine of St. Stanislaus.

Stroll through the Cloth Hall, the grand Renaissance trading arcade that has occupied the centre of the Main Square since the 14th century. The ground floor maintains its trading tradition with stalls selling amber jewellery, carved wooden crafts and Polish souvenirs, while the upper gallery houses a branch of the National Museum. This iconic structure perfectly embodies Kraków's medieval prosperity as a major trading centre.

Descend into the Wieliczka Salt Mine, a UNESCO World Heritage Site just outside Kraków where salt has been extracted continuously since the 13th century. The underground chambers entirely carved from rock salt include ornate chapels, elaborate sculptures and even an underground lake, all lit by crystal chandeliers also made from salt. This subterranean marvel, reaching 327 metres deep, showcases centuries of mining history and remarkable artistic achievement.

Pay solemn tribute at the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial and Museum, the largest Nazi concentration camp where over one million people perished during the Second World War. This harrowing site preserves physical evidence of genocide through the original barracks, guard towers and crematoria that bear witness to unimaginable human tragedy. This experience, though emotionally demanding, provides essential historical education about the Holocaust.

activities.text: The Planty Park encircles the historic centre for 4km, occupying the site of medieval ramparts demolished between 1806 and 1812. An evening stroll through this tree-lined boulevard makes the perfect end to your first day, with the illuminated facades of the Old Town as your backdrop.

Plac Nowy has been the heart of Kazimierz life since the 19th century. The brick rotunda at its centre once served as a kosher chicken market; today its stalls serve zapiekanka, a toasted baguette half topped with mushrooms, cheese and various garnishes, considered by locals to be Polish pizza. The atmosphere of the square, with its bars and outdoor cafés, differs from that of the Old Town - less touristy, more alive.

The afternoon is free for exploring Kraków. MOCAK (Museum of Contemporary Art), installed in a building constructed on the former Schindler factory site, has showcased Polish and international art since 2011. Another option: the Podgórze district across the Vistula, where a fragment of the wall from the former Jewish ghetto created by the Nazis in 1941 still stands. The Ghetto Museum occupies Tadeusz Pankiewicz's pharmacy - he was the only non-Jew permitted to remain in the ghetto.

Stary Kleparz market, a few minutes' walk from the Old Town, has operated since the Middle Ages. This is where Kraków locals still do their shopping: sheep's cheeses, dried mushrooms, artisan cold cuts, mountain honey. An ideal spot for last-minute purchases before the airport. The Kraków-Balice journey takes approximately 30 minutes by taxi or bus.

map_keywords: Main Market Square Rynek Główny Kraków

visits.text: Łańcut Castle passed from the Lubomirskis to the Potockis in the early 19th century. Built between 1629 and 1642 by Stanisław Lubomirski in the 'palazzo in fortezza' style, it was extensively remodeled as a neo-baroque residence in the late 19th century. You can visit some eighty preserved interiors, including the library of 22,000 volumes and the Great Hall. The English-style park covers 31 hectares.

The castle Stables house a collection of over 55 carriages and horse-drawn vehicles from the Potocki era—one of Europe's most important. Berlines, calashes, winter sleighs and Viennese harnesses are displayed in the neo-baroque buildings designed by French architect Armand Bauqué.

Łańcut's baroque synagogue, built in 1761 on the site of an earlier wooden structure, stands a few minutes' walk from the castle. It's one of the rare four-pillar synagogues still standing in Poland. The Potocki family financed its construction.

activities.text: Strolling through the castle's romantic park.
Classical music concert in the salons.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Marvel at the Słowiński Sand Dunes, the tallest moving dunes in Europe, creating an otherworldly desert landscape within Słowiński National Park. These remarkable formations shift approximately 10 meters annually, gradually burying the surrounding pine forests in their inexorable advance. Walking across these vast sandy expanses, with the Baltic Sea glittering in the distance, provides a truly unique natural experience.

Marvel at the moving sand dunes of Słowiński National Park, where vast expanses of shifting sands create an almost desert-like landscape on the Baltic coast. These spectacular dunes, constantly reshaped by wind and waves, are among the most impressive natural phenomena in northern Europe. Walking trails lead through this unique ecosystem where land and sea meet in dramatic fashion.

Encounter life-size dinosaurs at the Dinosaur Park in Łeba, where animatronic prehistoric creatures move and roar just as they might have millions of years ago. The park combines entertainment with education through additional attractions including a rope park, mini zoo, 5D cinema and exciting playgrounds. This immersive experience stimulates young imaginations while teaching about Earth's fascinating prehistoric past.

activities.text: Explore Słowiński National Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve protecting a remarkable coastal ecosystem of shifting dunes, pristine lakes, and diverse wildlife habitats. The park's unusual landscapes include dune fields, coastal forests, and wetlands teeming with migratory birds. Hiking trails and observation platforms allow visitors to appreciate this dynamic environment where sand, water, and wind constantly reshape the terrain.

Relax in the seaside resort of Łeba, a charming Baltic town serving as the gateway to the moving dunes. This former fishing village combines beach holiday amenities with traditional Polish coastal character. Horseback riding excursions across the sandy landscapes offer a memorable way to experience this unique coastal environment.
Explore the unique shifting dune landscapes and coastal ecosystems of Słowiński National Park on foot or by bike

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Kazimierz Dolny deserves a stop on the Białowieża-Lublin route. This Renaissance village on the Vistula has been dubbed the 'Polish Montmartre': painters and writers have been coming here since the early 20th century. The market square is surrounded by the carved houses of the Przybyła brothers (early 17th century), castle ruins crown the hill, and medieval granaries line the alleys. Two hours suffice for a lunch stop and a stroll.

It was in Lublin Castle, in 1569, that the Union of Lublin was signed—the founding act of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth uniting the Kingdom of Poland and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. The Chapel of the Holy Trinity, built in the 14th century, houses Byzantine-Ruthenian frescoes completed in 1418 at the behest of King Władysław Jagiełło. Long covered with plaster, they were rediscovered in 1897 and restored over nearly a century—they're considered Poland's most remarkable medieval frescoes.

Lublin's Old Town retains 70% of its original buildings, from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance. The Kraków Gate (14th century), the Great Market Square with the old town hall, the Dominican Basilica and the baroque Cathedral of St. John the Baptist form a coherent ensemble. The Grodzka Gate once marked the boundary between the Christian town and the Jewish quarter.

The Majdanek memorial stands 4 km from the city center. This Nazi concentration camp, operational from 1941 to 1944, is one of Europe's best preserved—barracks, watchtowers and crematorium are visible. Entry is free. The visit is sober and unstaged: the place speaks for itself.

Kozłówka Palace, 35 km from Lublin, houses two unusual collections under one roof: Count Zamoyski's apartments, preserved in their late-19th-century state, and a museum of Soviet socialist realism—portraits of Stalin, busts of Lenin, propaganda posters—salvaged after 1989. The juxtaposition is dizzying.

activities.text: Exploring Lublin's underground network: 280 meters of medieval cellars connect 14 buildings beneath the Old Town. This fitted-out route tells the story of the city's merchant history in the 15th-17th centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Stand in awe before Malbork Castle, the largest castle in the world by surface area and a UNESCO World Heritage masterpiece of medieval brick Gothic architecture. This colossal fortress served as the headquarters of the Teutonic Knights who ruled vast territories of Northern Europe from these imposing walls. The castle complex includes the High Castle, Middle Castle and Lower Castle, each revealing layers of medieval political power and religious devotion.

Explore the castle's magnificent interior chambers, including the Grand Master's Palace with its stunning vaulted ceilings and the atmospheric cellars and passages that wind beneath the fortress. The castle museum displays medieval armor, weapons and artifacts that bring the Teutonic era vividly to life. Evening sound and light shows illuminate the castle walls, creating an unforgettable spectacle against the night sky.

activities.text: Walk the castle's extensive defensive walls and towers for panoramic views over the Nogat River and surrounding countryside

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Stroll along Międzyzdroje's famous Promenade of Stars, where handprints of beloved Polish movie stars are embedded in the pavement like a Baltic Hollywood Walk of Fame. This elegant seaside resort boasts one of the most beautiful beaches on the Polish coast, with fine sand stretching beneath dramatic coastal cliffs. The historic pier extends far into the Baltic Sea, offering romantic sunset views and fresh sea breezes.

Explore the Baltic Miniature Park where meticulously crafted 1:25 scale models showcase the most important monuments of Baltic countries. Each miniature faithfully reproduces architectural details of famous landmarks, creating a fascinating geography and history lesson in one enchanting location. The park also features playgrounds, a miniature train ride and family-friendly amenities.

Journey back to the Middle Ages at the Slavs and Vikings Centre in Wolin, an archaeological open-air museum recreating life over a thousand years ago. Visitors can explore reconstructed huts, workshops and authentic equipment used by the Slavic and Viking peoples who once inhabited this region. Craft demonstrations, warrior combat shows and educational workshops bring ancient history dramatically to life.

Visit Wolin National Park to encounter European bison at the renowned Bison Show Farm, where these majestic animals can be observed up close along with deer and wild boars. The park features numerous hiking trails, scenic viewpoints and educational paths that wind through pristine coastal forests. The Natural History Museum presents the region's diverse fauna and flora through engaging dioramas and interactive exhibitions.

activities.text: Walk the educational trails of Wolin National Park to discover the unique ecosystems of this protected coastal wilderness

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau memorial comprises two sites 3km apart: Auschwitz I (the former Polish camp converted into a concentration camp in 1940, now the main museum) and Auschwitz II-Birkenau (the largest extermination camp built from 1941). The Soviet Red Army liberated the site on 27 January 1945, a date since recognized as International Holocaust Remembrance Day. The guided tour lasts a minimum of 3.5 hours. Entry is free; guided tours are charged (approximately 100 PLN). Booking required at auschwitz.org, several weeks in advance during high season.

activities.text: Time for contemplation and personal reflection.
Visit to the historic centre of Oświęcim.

days_plan.itin_title: Kraków, Tatras & Memory: Southern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Żelazowa Wola lies 46 km west of Warsaw, on the Masovian plain. Frédéric Chopin was born there on March 1, 1810 - his parents left the village that same autumn. The birthplace, a former outbuilding of the Skarbek manor, has been transformed into a museum. The park, crossed by a stream and planted with willows, houses five Chopin monuments. Open-air piano recitals are held every Saturday and Sunday from May to September, at noon and 3 PM, included in the ticket. Closed Mondays.

Płock overlooks the Vistula from a 50-meter cliff. Its diocese was founded in 1075; from 1079 to 1138, the Dukes of Poland resided there, making it the country's political center. The Romanesque cathedral, built between 1130 and 1144 by Bishop Alexander of Malonne (from the Namur region), houses the tombs of Vladislaus I Herman and Bolesław III Wrymouth. Tumskie Hill (Wzgórze Tumskie) offers panoramic views over the Vistula and the flat Masovian lands.

activities.text: Kampinos National Park, adjacent to Żelazowa Wola, covers 385 km² of lowland forests between Warsaw and Żelazowa Wola - one of the rare national parks in the immediate vicinity of a European capital. Lynx populations have been reintroduced there. The time available between Warsaw and Żelazowa Wola allows for a brief walk on marked trails along the inland dunes.

days_plan.itin_title: Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula

visits.text: Gather in Poznań's magnificent Old Market Square to witness the famous mechanical goats that emerge from the Town Hall clock tower at noon each day to butt heads twelve times. This beloved 16th-century tradition draws crowds daily to the heart of one of Poland's oldest and most historic cities. The surrounding Renaissance merchant houses with their colorful façades create a quintessential Central European townscape.

Explore the Gothic Town Hall, a Renaissance architectural masterpiece housing the fascinating Museum of the History of Poznań. The building's elegant arcaded loggia and ornate interiors reflect the prosperity of this important trading center during its golden age. Climbing the tower rewards visitors with sweeping views across the historic Old Town and modern city beyond.

Visit the Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul on Ostrów Tumski (Cathedral Island), the oldest cathedral in Poland and spiritual birthplace of the Polish nation. This historic island in the Warta River holds the remains of Poland's first rulers and witnessed the country's conversion to Christianity in 966 AD. The cathedral's chapels, particularly the Golden Chapel containing royal tombs, showcase centuries of religious art and national history.

activities.text: Discover Poznań's reputation as a vibrant university city with a youthful energy that fills its cafés, bars, and cultural venues. The city's numerous students bring vitality to historic streets where intellectual traditions date back centuries. This dynamic atmosphere makes Poznań one of Poland's most engaging urban destinations beyond its considerable architectural attractions.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure

visits.text: The Sanok skansen is Poland's largest open-air folk architecture museum and Europe's second largest, with over 180 wooden buildings across 38 hectares. The structures were moved from their original villages, restored to their initial state, and recreate four distinct ethnographic worlds: Boyko and Lemko villages in the upper section, Pogórzanie and Doliniane lower down. Each sector has its period Orthodox or Greek Catholic church, its period-furnished houses, its forges and water mills.

Sanok's Royal Castle, dominating the town from a hilltop, houses a collection of Byzantine and Greek Catholic icons from the Carpathians, one of Poland's most important. An entire room is devoted to the work of Zdzisław Beksiński (1929-2005), a painter born in Sanok, known for his hallucinatory post-apocalyptic landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Enter the magical world of Hobbit Village in Sieraków Sławieński, a unique attraction inspired by the fantastical universe of J.R.R. Tolkien's beloved novels. Children can explore authentic-looking hobbit houses built into the hillside, participate in outdoor games and join creative handicraft workshops. Themed parties, picnics and bonfires create an enchanting atmosphere that stimulates imagination and adventure.

Visit Pomerania Amusement Park in nearby Dygowo, offering attractions for children of all ages from gentle carousels to exciting rides. The park features trains, slides, a rope course and bouncy castles alongside a dedicated toddler zone with safe, age-appropriate play equipment. Organized animations, competitions and artistic performances ensure non-stop entertainment throughout the day.

activities.text: Dress comfortably for outdoor adventures in the Hobbit Village and prepare for field games and creative activities

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Sopot's pier extends 511.5 meters into the Baltic Sea. The first structure was built in 1827 by Dr. Jerzy Haffner; the pier reached its current length through successive expansions. It's the longest wooden pier in Europe. In season, access is ticketed. Views stretch to Gdańsk and Gdynia from the far end.

The Crooked House (Krzywy Domek, 2004), at 53 Monte Cassino Street, is a commercial building whose undulating facade was inspired by the illustrations of Polish storyteller and artist Jan Marcin Szancer. It's part of the Rezydent shopping center. The surreal architecture contrasts with the Belle Époque and Art Nouveau villas along the main boulevard.

activities.text: The pedestrian street Bohaterów Monte Cassino, linking the train station to the sea, concentrates boutiques, restaurants, and terraces. Sopot has been the seaside resort of aristocracy and haute bourgeoisie since the late 19th century—Germany's Wilhelm II and numerous European sovereigns vacationed here. The Grand Hotel (1927, neoclassical style) borders the beach north of the pier. The Forest Opera (Opera Leśna), set in the woods behind the town, hosts open-air concerts every summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Polish Baltic Coast: Gdańsk, Sopot & the Teutonic Fortress

visits.text: Tykocin, on the route between Białystok and Supraśl, warrants an hour's stop. Its baroque synagogue of 1642—one of Poland's best preserved—stands on a square virtually unchanged for three centuries. Inside, Hebrew inscriptions cover the walls from floor to ceiling. The contrast between this silence and the bustle of modern Białystok is striking.

In Supraśl, the Monastery of the Annunciation was founded in 1498 by Marshal of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania Aleksander Chodkiewicz. The main church, built between 1503 and 1511, was destroyed by the German army in 1944 and reconstructed over 40 years—it wasn't consecrated again until 2021. The Palace of the Archimandrites (1635-1655) houses the Icon Museum with some 400 pieces. The baroque buildings from the 17th-18th centuries remain in monastic use.

activities.text: The village of Supraśl is surrounded by the Knyszyn Forest. A bike ride or walk on the marked forest trails offers a gradual introduction to the atmosphere of Podlachia's great forests, before tomorrow's primeval forest of Białowieża.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Discover the Świdwie Nature Reserve, a paradise for birdwatchers and nature enthusiasts located in pristine wetlands near Szczecin. This protected area is home to remarkable bird species including cranes, storks and majestic white-tailed eagles that nest in the marshlands. Educational trails and observation towers provide comfortable viewing platforms to witness these magnificent creatures in their natural habitat.

Ascend the Świnoujście Lighthouse, the tallest lighthouse on the entire Polish coast and one of the highest in the world. The climb to the top presents a rewarding challenge, with breathtaking panoramic views of the city, the bustling port and the endless Baltic Sea as your prize. This iconic maritime beacon has guided ships safely into harbor for over 160 years.

Step back in time at Fort Gerhard, where interactive tours led by guides in authentic 19th-century soldier costumes bring military history vividly to life. This well-preserved fortress offers a fascinating glimpse into the strategic importance of this coastal location and the soldiers who once defended it. The tunnels, ramparts and gun emplacements create an adventurous atmosphere perfect for young explorers.

Visit Lake Turquoise in nearby Wapnica, a stunning reservoir with intensely turquoise-colored water created on the site of a former chalk mine. This picturesque lake offers walking paths through extraordinary landscapes where the vibrant water colors create an almost surreal natural spectacle. Viewpoints and picnic areas provide perfect spots to absorb the unique beauty of this geological wonder.

activities.text: Bring binoculars and cameras to capture the diverse wetland ecosystems and learn about the ecological importance of these protected marshes
Walk along the scenic trails surrounding Lake Turquoise to capture unique photographs of its remarkable turquoise waters

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Explore the magnificent Szczecin Castle, the Renaissance-style former residence of the Dukes of Pomerania that dominates the city's skyline. This impressive fortress complex houses museums, concert halls and courtyards that tell the story of the region's noble heritage. The castle's towers offer panoramic views over the Oder River and the surrounding historic quarter.

Marvel at St. Jacob's Cathedral, one of the largest brick Gothic churches in the entire region, whose soaring spire was only recently restored to its former glory. The cathedral's imposing interior features magnificent vaulted ceilings and medieval artwork that showcase the architectural prowess of Hanseatic craftsmen. This sacred space has witnessed centuries of prayer and devotion since the Middle Ages.

Admire the award-winning Philharmonic Hall, a stunning contemporary building that opened in 2015 and has garnered international acclaim for both its architecture and exceptional acoustics. The building's angular white facade creates a striking contrast with the city's historic structures and represents Poland's modern cultural renaissance. Concert-goers from across Europe come to experience performances in this acoustic masterpiece.

Stroll along the Hakenterrasse (Wały Chrobrego), a magnificent ensemble of early 20th-century buildings perched above the Oder River. This elegant promenade offers spectacular views of the waterfront, the port and the ships passing by below. The terraced gardens and monumental architecture create one of the most photographed panoramas in all of Pomerania.

activities.text: Challenge yourself at Tarzania Rope Park, where various courses with different difficulty levels allow visitors of all ages to test their agility among the treetops
Visit the Museum of Technology and Communication to discover vintage cars, motorcycles, trams and historic computing equipment with hands-on interactive exhibitions

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: The Kopernik factory has been producing Toruń gingerbread since 1763, founded by Jan Weese. The official recipe is a secret guarded by only six people: cinnamon, ginger, cloves, and pepper are confirmed, the rest is protected. The dough matures for several months in cellars before being baked—like wine, says the company spokesman. Frédéric Chopin, passing through Toruń in the 1820s, wrote to his family praising these biscuits. The factory tour includes a tasting of the complete range.

The Living Museum of Gingerbread (Żywe Muzeum Piernika) is housed in the former Weese factory, on Strumykowa Street. The 75-minute session takes place in medieval costume: you learn to press the dough into a hand-carved wooden mold, unmold, bake, and decorate with white icing. The molds depict knights, angels, carriages. The gingerbread you make goes home with you. The museum also documents 600 years of local production history.

activities.text: Medieval dinner in one of the Gothic cellars of Toruń's old town: dark beer soup (piwo ciemne), spit-roasted sausage, black bread, and aged cheese. Toruń's old town has been a UNESCO site since 1997 and holds an exceptional concentration of 14th-century Gothic brickwork. Nicolaus Copernicus was born here in 1473—that's why the Kopernik factory bears his name.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Culinary Poland: From Tatra Pierogi to Baltic Herring

places.name: Warsaw

visits.text: Warsaw's Old Town (Stare Miasto) was 85% destroyed during World War II and rebuilt brick by brick using the 18th-century vedute paintings of Bernardo Bellotto. This feat earned it UNESCO inscription in 1980. The Royal Castle, reconstructed between 1971 and 1984 thanks to donations from Polish citizens, houses the Bellotto Room - 23 paintings that served as blueprints for the rebuilders. The apartments of Stanisław August Poniatowski, Poland's last king, are recreated identically. The castle is free on Wednesdays.

The Royal Route (Krakowskie Przedmieście then Nowy Świat) connects the castle to baroque residences: the Presidential Palace, the university founded in 1816, St. Anne's Church. On Nowy Świat, the Blikle patisserie (no. 33) has served pączki since 1869. Charles de Gaulle, who lived in the neighboring building during his 1919-1920 stay in Warsaw, was a customer of the establishment.

The Warsaw Uprising Museum opened on July 31, 2004 for the 60th anniversary of the uprising. On August 1, 1944, some 20,000 members of the Home Army attacked the German garrison; the city held out for 63 days. The museum recreates these weeks of urban combat through original objects, sound recordings, period films and multimedia installations. The uprising and its aftermath - 200,000 deaths, deliberate destruction of the city by the Nazis - were long censored under the communist regime.

The POLIN Museum (Museum of the History of Polish Jews) opened on April 19, 2013 for the 70th anniversary of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising. The building, designed by Finnish architects Rainer Mahlamäki and Ilmari Lahdelma, is built on the site of the ghetto. The permanent exhibition covers 1,000 years of Jewish history in Poland, from the Middle Ages to the present. POLIN was voted best European museum in 2016.

Wilanów Palace was built between 1677 and 1696 as the summer residence of John III Sobieski, victor over the Turks at Vienna in 1683. The architecture combines French baroque and Polish ornamentation - facades covered in stucco bas-reliefs. The French gardens, terraces and baroque orchard extend over several hectares behind the palace. The palace houses collections of Polish and European paintings, including royal portraits.

The Praga district occupies the east bank of the Vistula, facing the Old Town. The only part of Warsaw preserved from Nazi destruction, it retains its pre-war buildings, cobbled courtyards and graffiti. The Neon Museum gathers illuminated signs from the communist era (1950-1989) - a collection of 200 neons recovered from Polish streets. Former factories have been converted into galleries, workshops and alternative cafes.

Explore Warsaw's meticulously reconstructed Old Town, a UNESCO World Heritage site that stands as a remarkable testament to Polish resilience. After suffering more than 85% destruction during World War II, the city was patiently rebuilt by determined citizens. The charming market square and surrounding streets now showcase the architectural beauty that once characterized this historic capital.

Visit the Royal Castle, the beautifully restored former official residence of Polish monarchs in its baroque splendor. This grand palace overlooking Castle Square contains sumptuously decorated state rooms, royal apartments and an impressive collection of paintings and decorative arts. The castle serves both as a museum and a symbol of Poland's royal heritage.

Discover the Warsaw Uprising Museum, an innovative and deeply moving tribute to the 1944 uprising against Nazi occupation. Through immersive exhibits, authentic artifacts and multimedia presentations, this award-winning museum tells the story of the 63-day struggle that shaped modern Polish identity. The experience provides essential context for understanding Warsaw's wartime destruction and subsequent rebirth.

Stroll through Łazienki Park, Warsaw's largest and most beautiful park, with the enchanting Palace on the Isle reflecting in tranquil waters. This 18th-century royal summer residence, surrounded by manicured gardens, peacocks and classical sculptures, offers a serene escape from urban exploration. The park's romantic landscapes and neoclassical architecture make it a favorite among locals and visitors alike.

Visit Wilanów Palace, often called the Polish Versailles, an exquisite baroque royal residence set amid formal gardens on the city's outskirts. Built as a summer retreat for King John III Sobieski, the palace showcases opulent interiors, period furniture and a remarkable collection of European art. The surrounding Italian and English gardens provide a picturesque setting for peaceful walks.

activities.text: Łazienki Park (76 hectares) is Warsaw's main royal park. The Palace on the Isle, built for King Stanisław August in the 18th century, reflects in an artificial lake. The statue of Chopin, sculpted by Henryk Kuna and unveiled in 1926, stands at the southern entrance - destroyed by the Nazis in 1940, it was recreated identically in 1958. Free Chopin concerts are held there every Sunday from May to September, at noon and 4 PM.

The Palace of Culture and Science was built in 1952-1955, Stalin's gift to Poland. At 237 meters and 42 floors, it long dominated central Warsaw. The observation deck on the 30th floor offers views of the city and its architectural paradoxes: reconstructed Old Town, Złote Tarasy, Soviet-era blocks and contemporary skyscrapers. Free on Wednesdays.

Dinner at a new Polish cuisine restaurant in Warsaw. The capital's gastronomic scene has transformed since 2015: chefs like those at Nolita or Senses revisit tradition - barszcz in different textures, pierogi with foie gras, carp gravlax-style. Tasting menus between 180 and 280 PLN without wine pairings. Reservation required.

On the way back to Warsaw, Puławy (30 km after Kazimierz) merits an hour's stop. The Czartoryski Palace park (early 19th century) extends in terraces above the Vistula. Princess Izabela Czartoryska had created there one of the first museums open to the public in Poland - part of the collections is now in the National Museum of Kraków.

Return to Warsaw in the morning. Mirowska Hall (a late 19th-century covered market) offers regional charcuterie, cheeses, dried mushrooms and vodka specialties for last-minute purchases. Nowy Świat and Grodzka Street have the capital's best-stocked Polish design shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Culinary Poland: From Tatra Pierogi to Baltic Herring

Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula
Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula
Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula
Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula

map_keywords: POLIN Muzeum Historii Żydów Polskich Warsaw

visits.text: Explore Wrocław's stunning Market Square, one of the largest and most beautiful in Europe, surrounded by elegant townhouses showcasing centuries of architectural styles. The ornate Gothic Town Hall dominates this vast public space where locals and visitors gather at outdoor cafés to soak up the vibrant atmosphere. With over 100 parks and green spaces, Wrocław ranks among Europe's greenest cities.

Hunt for Wrocław's famous bronze dwarfs, over 300 whimsical small sculptures scattered throughout the city streets, each with its own character and story. This beloved urban art project began as anti-communist symbolism and evolved into a charming citywide treasure hunt. The quirky figures appear in unexpected locations, delighting visitors who stumble upon these miniature bronze inhabitants.

Visit the Racławice Panorama, a monumental 360-degree painting depicting the 1794 battle where Polish peasant forces defeated the Russian army. This enormous circular canvas, measuring 15 by 114 meters, immerses viewers in the dramatic scene through innovative 19th-century presentation techniques. The painting's journey-hidden during Communist times-adds another layer to its historical significance.

activities.text: Cruise the Oder River to appreciate Wrocław's unique position across more than 100 bridges connecting its many islands. The city's waterways and bridges create romantic vistas at every turn, particularly beautiful when illuminated at night. This aquatic geography earned Wrocław comparisons to Venice and Amsterdam, though its character remains distinctly Central European.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure

visits.text: Krupówki Street is Zakopane's main artery. The wooden architecture in "Zakopane style" lining it was theorized in the late 19th century by Stanisław Witkiewicz, who sought a national aesthetic rooted in góral (highlander) traditions. The wooden church at Jaszczurówka (1904-1907), designed by Witkiewicz himself, stands as the finest example of this style. The adjacent market sells oscypek - smoked sheep's milk cheese from pine wood, protected by European PDO since 2008.

Arrive in Zakopane, Poland's premier mountain resort nestled at the foot of the spectacular Tatra Mountains. This charming town, known as Poland's winter capital, enchants visitors with its distinctive wooden architecture, vibrant highland culture and breathtaking alpine scenery. The pedestrian main street lined with traditional timber buildings showcases the unique regional style that developed in this isolated mountain community.

Ride the funicular railway up Mount Gubałówka for breathtaking panoramic views across Zakopane and the dramatic Tatra peaks beyond. The summit ridge offers easy walking trails, souvenir stalls selling local crafts, and traditional mountain food to sample while taking in the mountain panoramas. On clear days, the jagged silhouette of the High Tatras creates an unforgettable alpine backdrop.

Hike to Morskie Oko, Poland's largest and most beautiful mountain lake, set in a glacial cirque surrounded by towering granite peaks. The 7.7-kilometre trail through Tatra National Park passes cascading streams and virgin pine forests before revealing the crystalline waters reflecting sheer rock walls. This iconic natural wonder, whose name means 'Eye of the Sea', represents the quintessential Tatra experience.

activities.text: The thermal baths aren't located in Zakopane itself but in the surrounding area. Termy Gorący Potok in Szaflary (12km) and the complexes at Bukowina Tatrzańska (20km) offer geothermal pools at 38-40°C, outdoors facing the Tatras. These facilities work best for late afternoon after the drive from Oświęcim. Allow 2 to 3 hours on site.

The Kasprowy Wierch cable car departs from Kuźnice (15 minutes from Zakopane centre) and climbs to 1,987m in two sections, changing at Myślenickie Turnie (1,354m). Total journey time is approximately 20 minutes. The summit station marks the border with Slovakia; in clear weather, the panorama encompasses the High Tatras on the Slovak side and the Podhale plain on the Polish side. From the summit, one recommended option is to walk down through Gąsienicowa Valley to Kuźnice (7.4km, 3h15, -1,000m descent, moderate trail). Cable car tickets are sold online at kolejkalinowa.pl and sell out fast in July-August. Expect temperatures 10-15°C lower than in town.

Alternative for those preferring to avoid elevation gain: Morskie Oko, the highest lake in the Polish Tatras (1,395m). The walk from Palenica Białczańska car park takes 2.5 hours each way (9km on paved road without technical climbing). The lake is surrounded by glacial cirques and rock walls. During high season, book parking online or arrive before 8am.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Kraków, Tatras & Memory: Southern Poland in 7 Days
Kraków, Tatras & Memory: Southern Poland in 7 Days
Culinary Poland: From Tatra Pierogi to Baltic Herring

visits.text: Every house in Zalipie is repainted each spring before Corpus Christi. The tradition dates back to the late 19th century: village women used whitewash to cover soot stains left by old chimneyless stoves, then gradually transformed this practical gesture into increasingly complex floral patterns. Today, some thirty houses, barns, wells and dog kennels are covered with floral compositions—in blue, red and white on a white background.

The farm of Felicja Curyłowa (1904-1974) is open to the public as a museum. This artist, central figure of the tradition, had painted every surface of her three-room house—walls, ceiling, furniture, crockery and textiles included. Her first known work dates from 1914, when she was ten years old. After her death, the Cepelia Foundation bought the farm and preserved it identically.

activities.text: The House of Painters (Dom Malarek) offers workshops on floral painting on wood or ceramics, open to visitors without reservation. It's also the right place to get a village map and spot the best-decorated houses. The annual Malowana Chata ('painted cottage') competition, which has existed since 1948, takes place on the weekend following Corpus Christi, usually in early June.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Founded in 1580 by Chancellor Jan Zamoyski, Zamość was designed all at once by the Paduan architect Bernardo Morando according to Italian theories of the 'ideal city.' Nothing has been added to the historic center in 440 years. The Great Market Square (Rynek Wielki) is surrounded by arcades and colorful-fronted Armenian houses; the town hall with its grand staircase occupies the center. Zamość has been a UNESCO site since 1992.

Zamość's Sephardic synagogue, built between 1610 and 1618, was Poland's northernmost Sephardic synagogue. Its founders came from the Ottoman Empire and Italy—Zamoyski had studied in Padua and attracted Armenian, Jewish and other merchants to his ideal city. It survived because the Nazis converted it into a carpentry workshop. Now restored, it hosts a permanent exhibition on the region's Jewish history.
Zamość's Renaissance fortifications withstood Cossack assaults and Swedish invasions of the 17th century. The bastions and ramparts are largely preserved and form a walking route around the Old Town.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
